directive: Phase 6 chose feature-flag per-rule gradual cutover. In-flight handling is already fully resolved by that design — no buffering or queuing mechanism is needed. P9 scope is: (1) assert that the per-rule flag read is synchronous and occurs before any dispatch I/O begins (close the TOCTOU window — read flag, then open connection, not the reverse); (2) add tests covering the flag-flip boundary: alerts whose flag was read before the flip complete to the old target; alerts whose flag is read after the flip route to the new target; no alert appears in both systems. Do not implement a separate buffering layer or queuing mechanism — the flag-read-before-dispatch guarantee is sufficient to meet all three acceptance criteria.
rationale: The Executor's open question is answered by the p6 arbitration decision: phase 6 chose feature-flag per-rule gradual cutover. Each alert reads the per-rule flag state at the moment dispatch begins and routes to exactly one system — there is no transition window in which an alert can hit both old and new systems. A flag flip between two successive alerts for the same rule is handled naturally: the in-flight alert completes to whichever target the flag named when it was read; the next alert reads the updated flag. This is the design's inherent property, not something p9 needs to add. P9's scope is therefore narrow: assert the flag read is synchronous and occurs before dispatch begins (no TOCTOU gap), and test the flag-flip boundary. The acceptance criteria (no loss, no double-dispatch, matches p6 design) are already satisfied by p6's design; p9 verifies and hardens it.
